O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK DOMAIN ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN: The supply chain risk profile is in active breach across its two most consequential KRIs. Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12,610M total spend — 12.6 percentage points above the breach threshold of 50% — meaning over $7.9Bn of annual procurement flows through suppliers with no alternative qualified source. The three single-source suppliers are TSMC (CPU semiconductors, $4,200M, Taiwan, 18-week lead time), Foxconn (Manufacturing-Assembly, $2,100M, China, 8-week lead time), and Quanta (ODM-Laptops, $1,600M, Taiwan, 6-week lead time). TSMC represents the largest single dependency and the longest recovery horizon: an 18-week lead time against a minimum inventory cover of 5.2 weeks means that any TSMC production disruption — whether from geopolitical, natural disaster, or financial stress — would halt CPU supply approximately 5 weeks after event onset with no short-term remedy. The stress model confirms a 5-month recovery timeline if a major disruption materialises, with revenue at risk of $9.1Bn and a 95th-percentile supply chain VaR of $2,634M.

Minimum inventory cover across the supplier base has fallen to 3.9 weeks, breaching the 4-week threshold. The 3.9-week figure is pinned to Quanta, which simultaneously carries the lowest financial health score of any single-source supplier (61, below the distress threshold of 65), the lowest OTD performance (88.7%), and the highest incoming rejection rate (1.2%). Quanta therefore meets both conditions of the escalation trigger: financial_health below 65 and inventory below 4 weeks concurrently. A Quanta failure would disrupt the ODM-Laptop product line with less than 4 weeks of buffer and no alternative ODM source available.

Geographic concentration amplifies this risk materially: 53.5% of spend is in Taiwan (TSMC and Quanta) and a further 21.8% in China (Foxconn), meaning 75.3% of spend is concentrated across two jurisdictions with elevated geopolitical tension between them. Any Taiwan Strait disruption event would simultaneously impair TSMC and Quanta, eliminating two of three single-source suppliers at once.

Among multi-source suppliers, Yangtze Memory [SUP008] carries the lowest financial health score in the entire base (55), though it is not single-source. Its distress flag warrants monitoring as financial deterioration could shift effective supply concentration upward if Yangtze's capacity contribution is lost.

The stress model identifies dual-sourcing as the primary lever ($382M potential saving) followed by inventory buffer investment ($305M), against a 24% probability of a demand shock with 15% impact. The organisation should prioritise qualification of alternative CPU semiconductor sources and ODM partners as the most consequential BCM gap.

O-02 CYBER AND IT: The cyber risk posture is in multi-KRI breach and deteriorating. Mean time to detect (MTTD) stands at 11 days, against a breach threshold of 10 days — meaning the organisation is currently operating beyond its own acceptable detection horizon. Combined with a mean time to respond of 18 days, the total exposure window for an active threat actor is approaching 30 days. Four security incidents have already been recorded this month, making the detection lag operationally consequential rather than theoretical.

Patch compliance is at 71%, against a breach threshold of 80%, with 8 critical vulnerabilities and 23 high vulnerabilities open for more than 30 days. The 8 critical open vulnerabilities are below the breach threshold of 10 but above amber (5), and the combination with 71% patch compliance means the attack surface is both large and poorly maintained. Privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days, creating a lateral movement risk if any of the 4 incidents this month involved credential compromise.

The IT RTO for the order management system is exactly 6 hours — the breach threshold — providing zero headroom against any unplanned outage. This means the organisation is permanently at the boundary of a BCM failure for order fulfilment without any margin for extended recovery.

MFA coverage at 87% leaves 13% of accounts without multi-factor authentication, a meaningful gap given the phishing attempt rate of 2.3% and 4 active incidents this month.

The most acute systemic gap is supplier_cyber_resilience_assess_pct at 0%: not a single supplier has been assessed for cyber resilience this period, while 38% of third-party vendors remain unassessed overall. Given that 62.6% of spend flows through three single-source suppliers — TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta — none of whom carry a cyber resilience assessment, the organisation has no visibility into the cyber posture of its most critical supply chain nodes. This meets the O-02 escalation trigger: 0% supplier cyber assessment with 4 active security incidents in the period.

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY: No safety incidents or regulatory product notices have been recorded year-to-date. This is the single most important data point in the quality domain and confirms no zero-tolerance escalation is required at this time. First-pass yield of 97.2% and a customer complaint rate of 0.12% indicate manufacturing process quality is broadly stable.

However, field failure rate is recorded at 0.019 in the dataset. If this is interpreted as 1.9% (i.e., the value as provided represents a percentage), it is a significant breach of the 0.10% breach threshold. If 0.019 represents a raw rate (i.e., 0.019%), it remains above the amber threshold of 0.05% — data units should be confirmed with the QMS team. Under either interpretation, field failure rate is flagged as breach-status per the KRI data provided, and is driving 1,842 warranty claims this month, representing a live and recurring EBITDA cost.

Recall readiness score of 72 is in amber territory, 2 points above the breach threshold of 70, and is trending deteriorating: the last recall simulation was conducted 8 months ago, meaning the organisation's ability to execute a product recall has not been validated in a materially long period. If field failures were to escalate to a safety-relevant event, the recall execution capability is unvalidated and close to a formal breach. This combination — elevated field failures, high warranty claims, degraded recall readiness, and an 8-month-old simulation — represents a forward risk that the current clean safety record does not negate.

Supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% is in amber (breach threshold 1.0%), and notably Quanta's incoming rejection rate of 1.2% — as reported in the supply chain data — is already above the breach threshold for supplier quality, reinforcing the dual financial and quality risk that Quanta presents.

O-04 TALENT: Three flight-risk employees are flagged in R&D, meeting the escalation trigger threshold. This is the most time-sensitive talent risk: R&D capability loss is difficult to replace quickly (average time-to-fill critical Engineering roles is 68 days, with a counter-offer acceptance rate of only 42% indicating retention interventions are failing more than half the time), and directly affects the organisation's product development pipeline and its ability to qualify alternative suppliers under O-01 remediation programmes.

Engineering attrition is running at 14.2% annualised (amber, breach at 18%), against an 8.2% compensation gap versus market. The 42% counter-offer acceptance rate confirms that compensation-based retention is not effective for the majority of departing Engineering staff, suggesting non-compensation factors (engagement score 67%, internal promotion rate 18%) are also driving attrition. IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% (amber, approaching the 12% amber threshold from below) is a secondary but connected concern given the cyber posture deterioration described in O-02.

Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days across all functions (breach threshold: 6), meaning the organisation is already operating below critical headcount in at least 7 positions. SVP succession plan coverage at 72% (amber, 2 points above the 70% breach threshold) means leadership continuity in senior roles is not adequately planned for, compounding the flight-risk exposure in R&D.

The compensation gap, low engagement, and high open-role count together indicate a structural talent retention problem that requires intervention beyond counter-offers — which the 42% acceptance rate confirms are insufficient as a standalone mechanism.

CROSS-DOMAIN SUMMARY: The operational domain faces a compound risk scenario in which each of the four risk areas is either in breach or amber, and several have direct amplifying relationships: cyber weakness (O-02) reduces visibility into supply chain node compromise; talent gaps (O-04) slow the remediation of supply concentration (O-01) and degrade security operations; Quanta's quality and financial deterioration links O-01 and O-03; and degraded recall readiness (O-03) would be further impaired by a cyber incident affecting QMS systems (O-02). The organisation requires immediate escalation action on the three triggered escalation conditions: Quanta's combined financial and inventory exposure, the R&D flight-risk concentration, and the zero-percent supplier cyber assessment against a backdrop of active incidents.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

Override reflects leading indicators not captured in lagging control effectiveness score. This is the single most important data point in the quality domain and confirms no zero-tolerance escalation is required at this time. First-pass yield of 97.2% and a customer complaint rate of 0.12% indicate manufacturing process quality is broadly stable.

However, field failure rate is recorded at 0.019 in the dataset. If this is interpreted as 1.9% (i.e., the value as provided represents a percentage), it is a significant breach of the 0.10% breach threshold. If 0.019 represents a raw rate (i.e., 0.019%), it remains above the amber threshold of 0.05% — data units should be confirmed with the QMS team. Under either interpretation, field failure rate is flagged as breach-status per the KRI data provided, and is driving 1,842 warranty claims this month, representing a live and recurring EBITDA cost.

Recall readiness score of 72 is in amber territory, 2 points above the breach threshold of 70, and is trending deteriorating: the last recall simulation was conducted 8 months ago, meaning the organisation's ability to execute a product recall has not been validated in a materially long period. If field failures were to escalate to a safety-relevant event, the recall execution capability is unvalidated and close to a formal breach. This combination — elevated field failures, high warranty claims, degraded recall readiness, and an 8-month-old simulation — represents a forward risk that the current clean safety record does not negate.

Supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% is in amber (breach threshold 1.0%), and notably Quanta's incoming rejection rate of 1.2% — as reported in the supply chain data — is already above the breach threshold for supplier quality, reinforcing the dual financial and quality risk that Quanta presents.

O-04 TALENT: Three flight-risk employees are flagged in R&D, meeting the escalation trigger threshold. This is the most time-sensitive talent risk: R&D capability loss is difficult to replace quickly (average time-to-fill critical Engineering roles is 68 days, with a counter-offer acceptance rate of only 42% indicating retention interventions are failing more than half the time), and directly affects the organisation's product development pipeline and its ability to qualify alternative suppliers under O-01 remediation programmes.

Engineering attrition is running at 14.2% annualised (amber, breach at 18%), against an 8.2% compensation gap versus market. The 42% counter-offer acceptance rate confirms that compensation-based retention is not effective for the majority of departing Engineering staff, suggesting non-compensation factors (engagement score 67%, internal promotion rate 18%) are also driving attrition. IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% (amber, approaching the 12% amber threshold from below) is a secondary but connected concern given the cyber posture deterioration described in O-02.

Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days across all functions (breach threshold: 6), meaning the organisation is already operating below critical headcount in at least 7 positions. SVP succession plan coverage at 72% (amber, 2 points above the 70% breach threshold) means leadership continuity in senior roles is not adequately planned for, compounding the flight-risk exposure in R&D.

The compensation gap, low engagement, and high open-role count together indicate a structural talent retention problem that requires intervention beyond counter-offers — which the 42% acceptance rate confirms are insufficient as a standalone mechanism.

CROSS-DOMAIN SUMMARY: The operational domain faces a compound risk scenario in which each of the four risk areas is either in breach or amber, and several have direct amplifying relationships: cyber weakness (O-02) reduces visibility into supply chain node compromise; talent gaps (O-04) slow the remediation of supply concentration (O-01) and degrade security operations; Quanta's quality and financial deterioration links O-01 and O-03; and degraded recall readiness (O-03) would be further impaired by a cyber incident affecting QMS systems (O-02). The organisation requires immediate escalation action on the three triggered escalation conditions: Quanta's combined financial and inventory exposure, the R&D flight-risk concentration, and the zero-percent supplier cyber assessment against a backdrop of active incidents.

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

# STRATEGIC RISK REPORT
**Generated by Strategic Risk Agent | Enterprise Risk Management System**

---

## 1. DOMAIN STATUS
The strategic risk portfolio is in a **critical posture**: S-01 Geopolitical is in formal KRI breach, S-02 AI Disruption is at amber warning, and S-03 M&A Integration is within acceptable bounds but carries active exposure across three concurrent deals.

---

## 2. S-01 FINDING — GEOPOLITICAL RISK | **BREACH**
Five geopolitical signals are active, of which four are high severity. The high-severity signals — **"US expands Entity List by 14 technology companies,"** **"Taiwan Strait military exercises increase in frequency,"** and **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM"** — combine supply-chain, military escalation, and competitive displacement pressures simultaneously. The medium-severity signals **"EU announces reciprocal tariff review on Chinese electronics"** and **"India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing"** indicate that regulatory and trade fragmentation is broadening beyond the US-China bilateral axis. The volume and severity concentration of this signal cluster constitutes the KRI breach.

---

## 3. S-02 FINDING — AI DISRUPTION RISK | **AMBER**
Three competitive signals are active in the AI disruption domain. **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM"** and **"HP announces partnership with Google for Gemini-embedded Chromebook Pro"** represent direct competitive moves by major hardware players embedding AI capabilities at the device level. **"India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing"** introduces an additional structural shift that may accelerate competitive entries in price-sensitive segments. The convergence of these three signals warrants active monitoring and has driven the amber status.

---

## 4. S-03 FINDING — M&A INTEGRATION RISK | **OK**
Three deals are active across different lifecycle stages: **CloudScale Systems** (ACQ001) is in Integration Month 18 at USD 1,200M, **NeuralEdge AI** (ACQ002) is in Due Diligence at USD 450M, and **DataVault Security** (ACQ003) is in Negotiation at USD 280M. Synergy delivery is tracking at **84.0%** against target, which supports the OK status. However, the portfolio carries USD 1,930M in combined deal value with two transactions not yet closed, representing meaningful execution exposure concurrent with the geopolitical breach environment.

---

## 5. TOP ACTIONS

| Risk | Top Action |
|---|---|
| **S-01 Geopolitical** | Convene an emergency supply-chain war-room to assess direct exposure to the 14 newly listed entities and map single-source dependencies in Taiwan-linked components. |
| **S-02 AI Disruption** | Accelerate the NeuralEdge AI (ACQ002) due-diligence timeline to determine whether on-device AI capabilities close the competitive gap identified in the Dell and HP signals. |
| **S-03 M&A Integration** | Conduct a focused synergy-recovery review for CloudScale Systems (ACQ001) at Month 18 to identify the gap between the 84.0% delivery rate and full target before the integration window closes. |

---

## 6. ESCALATION
**YES** — S-01 Geopolitical KRI is in confirmed breach. Escalation to executive leadership and the Board Risk Committee is required.

F-01.agent_findings:
## Financial Risk Domain Assessment — 2026-05-28

### F-01 — FX & Commodity Risk

**Status: AMBER | Escalation Triggered**

FX-only unhedged exposure stands at USD 4,080M against an amber threshold of USD 4,000M and a breach threshold of USD 5,000M. The portfolio average FX hedge ratio is 46.3%, below the 60% amber threshold and within 1.3 percentage points of the 45% breach threshold. No individual FX position achieves the 60% amber hedge ratio threshold:

- **FX001 USD_CNY (Revenue, JPMorgan):** Gross USD 3,200M, hedged USD 1,400M (43.8% — below amber 60% threshold, within 1.2pp of breach at 45%). Unrealised P&L USD -8.4M. Maturity 6 months. Largest single unhedged revenue exposure at USD 1,800M.
- **FX002 USD_EUR (Revenue, Deutsche Bank):** Gross USD 2,800M, hedged USD 1,200M (42.9% — below amber threshold, within 0.1pp of 45% breach threshold on hedge ratio). Unrealised P&L +USD 7.2M. Maturity 12 months. Only position with positive unrealised P&L.
- **FX003 USD_JPY (Cost, HSBC):** Gross USD 980M, hedged USD 600M (61.2% — only position above amber threshold). Unrealised P&L USD -15.6M. Maturity 3 months — nearest-term maturity risk. Largest negative unrealised P&L of all FX positions.
- **FX004 USD_KRW (Cost, Citibank):** Gross USD 620M, hedged USD 320M (51.6% — below amber threshold). Unrealised P&L USD -9.8M. Maturity 6 months.

FX-only unrealised P&L totals USD -26.6M. Commodity positions (COM001 DRAM/Goldman Sachs USD -12.3M; COM002 NAND/JPMorgan USD -8.1M) contribute an additional USD -20.4M, bringing total portfolio unrealised P&L to USD -47.0M. Per CF-04 scope definition, the F-01 KRI references FX-only figures; commodity figures are carried separately for EBITDA covenant stress analysis.

The USD_EUR hedge ratio of 42.9% is critically close to the 45% breach threshold. The USD_CNY position at 43.8% is the largest gross exposure in the portfolio. Counterparty concentration is noted: JPMorgan appears in both FX001 and COM002.

Escalation is triggered: unhedged FX exposure of USD 4,080M exceeds the USD 500M escalation threshold.

---

### F-02 — Revenue Concentration

**Status: BREACH (bad_debt) / AMBER (concentration)**

**Top Customer Concentration:** 22.1% (Enterprise Tier1), above the 20% amber threshold and below the 25% breach threshold. Status: amber.

**Bad Debt Provision:** 1.44% portfolio-wide, against an amber threshold of 0.40%, breach threshold of 0.55%, and COV006 covenant threshold of 0.80%. Status: confirmed breach at both the KRI level and the covenant level. The provision has reached 2.62× the breach threshold.

Segment-level bad debt provisions by segment:
- Enterprise Tier1: USD 12.4M provision; USD 42M overdue 60d, USD 8M overdue 90d
- Enterprise Tier2: USD 28.6M provision; USD 68M overdue 60d, USD 18M overdue 90d
- SMB Tier3: USD 32.1M provision; USD 38M overdue 60d, USD 22M overdue 90d — highest 90d overdue relative to segment size
- Government Tier1: USD 2.8M provision; USD 4M overdue 60d, USD 0M overdue 90d — lowest credit risk segment
- Consumer Tier4: USD 18.4M provision; USD 24M overdue 60d, USD 12M overdue 90d

Total 90d overdue: USD 60.0M (context figure). Total bad debt provision: USD 94.3M (derived as sum across segments; this figure is the full COV006 cure cost in a complete write-off scenario).

The Enterprise Tier1 concentration at 22.1% represents the single most material credit concentration. Any deterioration in this customer's payment profile — given USD 50M already in overdue 60d+ — would exacerbate the existing COV006 breach and further compress EBITDA.

---

### F-03 — Liquidity & Refinancing

**Status: BREACH (COV006) / AMBER (COV001, COV004) / OK (COV002, COV003, COV005)**

#### COV006 — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH (Trade-Finance Facility)
Bad debt provision at 1.44% vs 0.80% threshold. Headroom: -0.64% (64bps over limit). This breach is confirmed and active — not probabilistic. Cure or formal waiver is required by **2026-06-12**.

**BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED — MANDATORY LENDER NOTIFICATION PROTOCOL:**
(a) Lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms. A formal board directive is required before any lender contact is initiated.
(b) Written board authorisation must be obtained and documented prior to Group Treasurer contacting lenders regarding the COV006 breach.
(c) Hard deadline for board resolution: **2026-06-12**.
(d) Named joint owners: **Group Treasurer AND CFO** — CFO co-signature is required per facility terms on all lender communications.
(e) If the notification obligation is not met before the **2026-06-30** test date, cross-default provisions may be triggered across other facilities.

**COV006 cure cost scenarios and COV001 impact:**
- Full write-off scenario: USD 94.3M charge flows through P&L → EBITDA falls to approximately USD 2.186B → COV001 EBITDA headroom reduced from USD 152M to USD ~58M (62% consumption).
- Partial cure scenario (USD 50M write-off): EBITDA falls to approximately USD 2.230B → COV001 headroom reduced to USD ~102M.
- Any cure strategy must be evaluated against its direct impact on COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA ratio.

#### COV001 — Net Debt/EBITDA (RCF-USD2B) — AMBER
Current ratio: 2.8x vs threshold 3.0x. Ratio headroom: 0.2x. Dollar headroom: USD 152M (computed: Net Debt USD 6.384B / 3.0x = USD 2.128B floor; current EBITDA USD 2.28B − USD 2.128B = USD 0.152B). Deteriorated from 2.6x at March 2026 test. Next test: 2026-06-30.

#### COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term Loan A) — OK
Current: 4.2x vs 3.5x threshold. Headroom: 0.7x. Compressed from 4.8x at March test — directional deterioration but no immediate concern.

#### COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B) — OK
Current: USD 1.1B vs USD 0.8B threshold. Headroom: USD 0.3B. Note: the escalation trigger is USD 0.9B — current USD 1.1B provides only USD 0.2B buffer above the escalation trigger. Compressed from USD 1.2B (March) to USD 1.1B (June). This KRI requires close monitoring.

#### COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027) — AMBER
Current: 8 months vs 12-month threshold. Headroom: 0 months within covenant definition. Runway deteriorated from 11 months (March) to 8 months (June). The 6-month escalation trigger is now 2 months away on current trajectory. Refinancing execution risk is elevated given the concurrent COV006 breach — lenders may seek COV006 cure as a precondition to refinancing discussions.

#### COV005 — EBITDA (All Facilities) — OK
Current: USD 2.28B vs USD 1.80B threshold. Headroom: USD 0.48B on absolute floor. However, the binding constraint is COV001 (as analysed above), which limits effective headroom to USD 152M.

#### Combined Stress Scenario
If total portfolio unrealised P&L of USD -47.0M (FX USD -26.6M + commodity USD -20.4M) were crystallised simultaneously with a full COV006 cure (USD 94.3M), total EBITDA impact would be approximately USD 141.3M — consuming 93% of the USD 152M COV001 headroom and leaving USD ~10.7M before COV001 breach. This combined stress scenario is not a base case but represents a credible tail scenario given current hedge ratios and bad debt trajectory.

---

### F-04 — Accounting & Financial Reporting

**Status: OK**

AUD003 Internal-SOX review (Financial Controls) is completed. Open high findings: 1. Critical findings: 0. Material weaknesses: 0. The amber threshold is 2 high findings; current count of 1 is below amber. No material weakness escalation trigger is met. The single open high finding warrants tracking to resolution but does not alter the overall status.

---

### Summary Observations

1. The COV006 breach is the most time-critical item in this assessment, with a hard cure/waiver deadline of 2026-06-12 and cross-default risk materialising at the 2026-06-30 test date.
2. The convergence of three simultaneously deteriorating F-03 covenants (COV001, COV004, COV006) and an F-01 hedge ratio approaching breach levels represents a compounding risk profile.
3. Liquidity headroom (COV003 at USD 1.1B) is 22% above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger — a thin buffer given the EBITDA compression pressures identified.
4. F-04 presents no material concerns at this time.

## COMPLIANCE RISK DOMAIN ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

### EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Two KRI breaches are active this period: (1) EU AI Act conformity assessment coverage at 78% against a ≥90% breach threshold with statutory deadline 2026-08-02, and (2) third-party anti-bribery and corruption (ABAC) coverage at 89% against a ≥95% breach threshold under FCPA and the UK Bribery Act 2010. No confirmed sanctions violations, GDPR violations, FCPA violations, or Bribery Act violations are recorded in the Legal Register this period. No formal regulatory investigation appears in audit_log.csv. Escalation thresholds under the zero-tolerance triggers are not met by the provided data, with the critical caveat noted under C-03 regarding undisclosed seniority of the AUD006 whistleblower high finding.

---

### C-01: EXPORT CONTROLS & SANCTIONS

**Coverage Status — AMBER**
Export screening coverage is 96.0% (source: Trade-Compliance-System), up from 95.0% in the prior data row. This places the KRI above the breach threshold of <95% but below the amber ceiling of <98%, sustaining an AMBER status. The improvement of 1 percentage point is directionally positive and the trend is assessed as improving. However, 4% of transactions remain unscreened — at a current monthly volume of 18,420 transactions screened, the unscreened population is estimated (by proportion, not fabricated) to be material in absolute terms. The obligation to screen 100% of transactions is not explicitly articulated with a named threshold in regulatory_horizon.csv for C-01 beyond DORA and NIS2 (which are IT-incident focused); the 95%/98% thresholds are provided as KRI operating parameters in the input data.

**Regulatory Gaps — DORA and NIS2**
regulatory_horizon.csv records two C-01-mapped regulations currently in Partial status:
- **DORA (EU), effective 2025-01-17**: Gap — IT incident response plan not formally tested. Owner: CISO. This regulation has been effective for over 16 months and the gap persists across both data rows in this run, indicating no remediation progress recorded in the horizon file. Note: IT testing assessment falls within Operational Agent authority (O-02); this agent flags the obligation mapping only.
- **NIS2 (EU), effective 2024-10-18**: Gap — Incident response plan not tested. Owner: CISO. Same structural gap, effective for over 19 months. Same scoping note applies.

Both DORA and NIS2 are listed under risk C-01 in regulatory_horizon.csv and are therefore mapped here, but the substantive control gap (untested incident response) is an operational security matter. This agent records the Partial compliance status and the prolonged duration of the gap without assigning a KRI value, as no C-01 KRI metric for DORA/NIS2 testing is supplied in kri_data.

**Sanctions Violations — OK**
confirmed_sanctions_violations_ytd = 0 (source: Legal-Register). No zero-tolerance trigger is met. Status: OK.

board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The organisation carries 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four risk domains simultaneously; the systemic risk flag is active, confirming concurrent multi-domain breach.

STRATEGICReplace 'The strategic domain records 1 breach and 2 amber warnings' with 'The strategic domain records 1 breach and 1 amber warning' (S-01 in breach; S-02 at amber; S-03 at low status with 84% synergy delivery vs 75% threshold)., and 3 active deals — CloudScale Systems, NeuralEdge AI, and DataVault Security — may shift customer revenue concentration in combination with financial exposures.

OPERATIONAL: The operational domain records 10 breaches and 7 amber warnings, accountable across COO, CISO, and CHRO by sub-domain. Supply chain single-source concentration stands at 62.6% against a 50% breach threshold, with zero percent of suppliers cyber-resilience assessed; MTTD of 11 days means compromise would likely be undetected until production impact materialises. Quanta [SUP005] carries the highest rejection rate at 1.2% and lowest financial health score of 61, threatening simultaneous supply interruption and quality deterioration against a 3.9-week inventory buffer. Recall readiness scores 72 — amber, with the last simulation 8 months ago — meaning a cyber incident could impair recall execution precisely when most needed. IT-Operations attrition of 11.8% and 7 critical open roles including IT functions directly degrade security operations capability, worsening MTTD and leaving 8 critical and 23 high vulnerabilities unaddressed. Three flight-risk employees in R&D and 7 critical open Engineering roles unfilled beyond 60 days weaken supplier qualification capacity, extending the 5-month supply recovery timeline.

FINANCIAL: The financial domain records 4 breaches and 7 amber warnings, accountable to the Chief Financial Officer. FX unhedged exposure stands at USD 4,080M with a portfolio hedge ratio of 46.3%; total unrealised portfolio P&L of USD -47.0M, if crystallised, would consume 81.3% of the USD 57.7M COV001 EBITDA headroom post-cure (gross headroom USD 152M less COV006 cure USD 94.3M). A partial COV006 bad-debt cure of USD 50M combined with crystallised market losses would erode USD 97M — 63.8% of available headroom — creating compound covenant stress where remedying one breach precipitates proximity to a second. Enterprise Tier1 top customer concentration sits at 22.1% against a 20% amber threshold, carrying USD 42M overdue 60 days and USD 8M overdue 90 days, with potential to accelerate bad-debt provisions against both COV006 and COV001 simultaneously. Bond-2027 maturity runway has deteriorated to 8 months from 11 months at the March test, now only 4 months above the 6-month escalation trigger; refinancing execution risk is elevated given the concurrent COV006 breach, as lenders may condition refinancing on its cure.

COMPLIANCE: The compliance domain records 2 breaches and 2 amber warnings, accountable to the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel. EU AI Act conformity assessment coverage stands at 78% against a ≥90% breach threshold with a statutory deadline of 2 August 2026; third-party ABAC coverage is in breach, and 3 denied party matches remain pending resolution, up from 2 in the prior period. AUD001 Tier-1 Supplier audit completed with 2 high-severity findings whose nature must be reviewed by the CCO to determine whether any involve sanctioned-country suppliers, given potential C-01 and O-01 interconnection. AUD006 Whistleblower audit records 1 high-severity finding; if any high finding involves SVP-level or above, an immediate zero-tolerance escalation trigger applies, and the CCO must confirm subject seniority without delay. AUD004 Privacy-GDPR completed with 1 high-severity finding; the CCO and Operational Agent must confirm whether flagged data-processing systems overlap with trade-compliance systems given potential O-02 implications.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential risk combination this period is the convergence of a confirmed covenant breach, a cyber control breakdown, and a supply chain in simultaneous multi-point failure — each severe in isolation, but mutually reinforcing in a way that makes the aggregate materially worse than the sum of parts. The COV006 lender covenant is confirmed in active breach today: the bad debt provision of 1.44% exceeds both the internal management action threshold of 0.55% and the COV006 lender covenant threshold of 0.80%. The Chief Financial Officer must present a lender notification and COV006 cure strategy to the Board by 12 June 2026. The headroom position is more constrained than the headline figures suggest: gross COV001 EBITDA headroom is USD 152 million, but the full COV006 cure provision of USD 94.3 million reduces effective headroom to USD 57.7 million; in the combined worst case, USD 47.0 million in total portfolio mark-to-market losses (FX and commodity positions combined) further compress that to USD 10.7 million before a COV001 breach. A conditional probability assessment — provisional pending a full model rerun by 20 June 2026 — places the likelihood of an additional COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA breach at the 30 June 2026 test date at more likely than not, at 50.5%. This is a separate and conditional figure from the COV006 certainty and must be presented to the Board as such. Alongside this financial stress, the cyber domain is in six simultaneous control breaches: mean time to detect stands at 11 days against a 10-day threshold, patch compliance at 71% against an 80% threshold, IT recovery time at exactly the 6-hour threshold limit with zero margin, MFA coverage at 87% against a 90% threshold, privileged access unreviewed for 45 days at the threshold limit, and supplier cyber resilience assessments at 0% against a 50% threshold. Simultaneously, supply chain single-source concentration is in breach at 62.6% against a 50% threshold, inventory cover has fallen to 3.9 weeks below the 4-week threshold, and geographic concentration is in breach at 75.3% against a 75% threshold. The board should treat these three domains as a compound stress, not three separate line items — the mechanisms connecting them are described in the section below.

CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most material cross-domain interaction this period runs between cyber resilience, supply chain concentration, and talent. Not a single one of the organisation's key single-source suppliers — TSMC, Foxconn, or Quanta — has received a cyber resilience assessment, at a moment when six internal cyber controls are simultaneously in breach and four security incidents have already been recorded this period. With mean time to detect at 11 days, a cyber intrusion originating from or propagating through any of these unassessed suppliers would almost certainly not be identified until production impact was already materialising — at which point the organisation has only 3.9 weeks of inventory cover before a supply interruption affects revenue. The talent domain closes the loop: seven critical engineering and sourcing roles have been open beyond 60 days, and three R&D employees are identified as flight risks, directly reducing the organisation's capacity to qualify alternative suppliers — the primary and most impactful remedy for the concentration breach. The stress model projects a five-month supply recovery timeline in a major disruption scenario; loss of key sourcing or engineering personnel extends that timeline further. A second critical interaction runs between the covenant position and the hedging programme. The commodity hedge ratio is in breach at 44.2% against a 45% threshold, and the FX hedge ratio is amber at 46.3% against a 60% policy target. Total portfolio mark-to-market losses of USD 47.0 million, if crystallised, would reduce post-cure COV001 effective headroom from USD 57.7 million to USD 10.7 million. The geopolitical signal count is in breach at five active signals — three of which are high severity, including US Entity List expansion and increased Taiwan Strait military activity — and a deteriorating geopolitical environment directly increases the probability of adverse currency and commodity moves crystallising against these unhedged positions. A third interaction connects cyber and talent to revenue continuity: the combination of IT-Operations attrition and seven critical open roles including IT functions is a direct driver of the deteriorating cyber posture, particularly MTTD and patch compliance. Flight-risk staff in revenue-critical technology functions, alongside elevated detection times, creates a scenario in which an active incident during a period of reduced security operations capacity could translate directly into extended revenue disruption — this is not an IT control failure in isolation but a potential revenue loss event. Finally, the three active M&A deals (CloudScale Systems, NeuralEdge AI, and DataVault Security, with two pre-close) add execution risk in the context of an organisation whose covenant headroom, talent capacity, and operational controls are simultaneously under stress; any integration-related distraction of senior management or financial resources compounds the pressure on all three domains concurrently.

QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from the prior period. Of 20 KRIs tracked across the comparison period, 15 are deteriorating, three are improving, and two are stable — an imbalance that confirms broad-based risk growth rather than isolated pockets. The dominant cause is inherent risk growth: the underlying threat environment has expanded regardless of control performance, most visibly in geopolitical signals rising from two to five active signals (amber to breach), inventory cover falling from 4.3 to 3.9 weeks (amber to breach), and the geopolitical and bad debt environments worsening independently of management action. Control weakening is also present — patch compliance has declined further from 78% to 71% (remaining in breach), supplier cyber resilience assessments have fallen from 15% to 0% (remaining in breach), tech attrition has risen from 12.5% to 14.2%, and recall readiness has deteriorated from 82% to 72% (moving from ok to amber) — but inherent risk growth is the dominant mechanism this period. Three KRIs have moved to new breach status this quarter: mean time to detect, critical open roles beyond 60 days, and inventory cover weeks. The single KRI whose reversal would most reduce residual risk this period is mean time to detect: at 11 days, it is the linchpin connecting an unassessed supply chain, a talent-depleted security function, and the potential for extended undetected exposure across an organisation under financial and operational stress simultaneously.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the CFO (owner: Group Treasurer / CFO) to notify the lending syndicate of the COV006 covenant breach immediately and present a Board-level cure strategy by 12 June 2026, as required by the action_deadline on the cross_default_risk KRI. The Committee must be informed that COV006 is confirmed in active breach today (certainty), that effective COV001 headroom post-cure is USD 57.7M (not the gross USD 152.0M), and that in the combined worst case — after USD 47.0M in portfolio mark-to-market losses — headroom narrows to USD 10.7M. The conditional probability of an additional COV001 breach at the 30 June 2026 test is more likely than not at 50.6% PROVISIONAL (full rerun due 20 June 2026); this figure must be reported to the Board as distinct from the COV006 certainty and will be updated once the rerun is complete.
  • Require the CISO to present an emergency remediation roadmap for the six concurrent cyber control failures in O-02 at the next Risk Committee meeting, with milestone dates for: restoring patch compliance from 71% to above the 80% threshold; reducing mean time to detect from 11 days to below 10 days; recovering MFA coverage from 87% to above 90%; and clearing the privileged access review backlog at the 45-day threshold limit. The Committee must also direct the Chief Operating Officer / VP Supply Chain to initiate supplier cyber resilience assessments immediately — the current 0% assessment rate against a 50% threshold, combined with 62.6% single-source supply concentration, means a cyber intrusion at any unassessed key supplier could simultaneously cause a security incident and a production disruption with 11 days of undetected exposure before response.
  • Direct the Chief Operating Officer / VP Supply Chain to present a formal dual-sourcing acceleration programme to the next Risk Committee meeting, addressing the breach-level single-source concentration of 62.6%, the sub-threshold inventory cover of 3.9 weeks, and the geographic concentration breach of 75.3%. The programme must include a supplier cyber assessment timetable covering 100% of critical single-source suppliers. Require the CHRO to co-present a critical role backfill plan for the 7 engineering and sourcing roles open beyond 60 days, as unfilled roles directly impede supplier qualification and extend supply recovery timelines; the Committee must note that loss of the 3 identified R&D flight-risk employees would further degrade this capacity.
  • Require the CFO / Group Treasurer to present a commodity hedging remediation plan to the Risk Committee, with the commodity hedge ratio in breach at 44.2% against a 45% threshold addressed as the immediate priority ahead of FX hedge ratio recovery toward the 60% policy target. The plan must quantify the covenant impact of continued unhedged exposure: in a stress scenario, combined FX and commodity market losses range from USD 924M to USD 1,258M, and USD 47.0M in mark-to-market losses would reduce effective COV001 headroom from USD 57.7M to USD 10.7M. The Committee must note the S-01+F-01 compound scenario: geopolitical signals have risen to 5 against a threshold of 3 and are deteriorating, increasing the probability of adverse currency and commodity moves crystallising against current unhedged positions.
  • Direct the Chief Compliance Officer / General Counsel to report to the Risk Committee on three open compliance matters before the next board pack is finalised: (1) confirm the seniority of the subject(s) of the high-severity AUD006 whistleblower finding — if any subject is at SVP level or above, an immediate escalation trigger applies under C-03 zero-tolerance rules and must be actioned without delay; (2) review the two high-severity findings from the AUD001 Tier-1 Supplier audit to determine whether any involve suppliers in sanctioned jurisdictions, given the C-01+O-01 interconnect risk with 3 denied party matches currently pending resolution; and (3) confirm whether data-processing systems flagged in the AUD004 privacy audit overlap with trade compliance systems, given the O-02 cyber posture. The Committee must note that third-party ABAC coverage remains in breach at 89% against a 95% threshold, and AI audit coverage, while improving, remains in breach at 78% against a 90% threshold.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 50.5% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× (model_params authoritative MC value using effective headroom USD 57.7M — 2026-06-09; original figure 69.4% was a calculation error corrected 2026-06-09) by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 21 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 50.5% means more likely than not that a COV001 breach will occur at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of USD 94.3M (corrected; prior USD 365M was a calculation error) recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 2,185.7M — USD 57.7M above the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — meaning the cure alone does not deterministically breach COV001, but the Monte Carlo shows P(breach) = 50.5% under plausible EBITDA scenarios at test date.
